meaning: Act of using violence to harm
exampleSentence: The army launched a surprise attack on the enemy base at dawn.
mnemonic: AT + TACK → Imagine pinning a TACK AT someone — that's an attack!

meaning: Reduce the force, effect, or value of
exampleSentence: The thick walls attenuate the noise from the busy street outside.
mnemonic: A-TEN-U-ATE → A TEN became an EIGHT — it got reduced!

meaning: Make (something) greater by adding to it; increase
exampleSentence: She decided to augment her income by taking a part-time tutoring job.
mnemonic: AUG-MENT → Think of AUGUST being the month things GROW (augment) before harvest.

meaning: Conducive to success; favourable
exampleSentence: The wedding was held on an auspicious day chosen by the family priest.
mnemonic: AU-SPICIOUS → Sounds like 'AUS-PRECIOUS' — gold (Au) is precious and favourable!

meaning: The right or condition of self-government
exampleSentence: The university grants its departments a high degree of autonomy in curriculum design.
mnemonic: AUTO-NOMY → AUTO means self, like an AUTOmatic car drives itself.

meaning: A strong dislike or disinclination
exampleSentence: He has a strong aversion to public speaking and avoids it whenever possible.
mnemonic: A-VERSION → Imagine seeing A bad VERSION of a movie — you'd have a strong dislike!

meaning: Keep away from or stop oneself from doing (something)
exampleSentence: She tried to avoid making eye contact with her former boss at the party.
mnemonic: A-VOID → There's a VOID (empty space) because you stayed away!

meaning: Very bad or unpleasant
exampleSentence: The weather was so awful that the outdoor concert had to be cancelled.
mnemonic: AWE-FUL → Originally meant 'full of awe' but now means terribly bad.

meaning: So lacking in originality as to be obvious and boring
exampleSentence: His banal remarks about the weather failed to impress anyone at the dinner.
mnemonic: BAN-ALL → BAN ALL boring, unoriginal things — they're banal!

meaning: A cause of great distress or annoyance
exampleSentence: Mosquitoes are the bane of every summer camping trip.
mnemonic: BANE rhymes with PAIN — the bane of your existence causes you pain!

meaning: The playful and friendly exchange of teasing remarks; as a verb, to exchange teasing remarks
exampleSentence: The colleagues enjoyed light banter over coffee every morning.
mnemonic: BAN-TER → Imagine a BAN on TERROR — only playful teasing allowed!

meaning: Savagely cruel; exceedingly brutal
exampleSentence: The barbaric treatment of prisoners shocked the international community.
mnemonic: BARBAR-IC → Barbarians were known for being savage and uncivilized.

meaning: Not clothed or covered
exampleSentence: The trees stood bare after shedding all their leaves in autumn.
mnemonic: BARE → A BEAR in the wild is bare — no clothes!

meaning: (Of land) too poor to produce much or any vegetation
exampleSentence: The barren desert stretched for miles with no sign of life.
mnemonic: BARREN → BAR + REN → Imagine a BAR in a barREN wasteland — nothing grows there.

meaning: A fence or other obstacle that prevents movement or access
exampleSentence: Language can be a significant barrier to communication in international business.
mnemonic: BARR-IER → A BAR across the road blocks your way — a barrier!

meaning: Pleasing the senses or mind aesthetically
exampleSentence: The beautiful sunset painted the sky in shades of orange and purple.
mnemonic: BEAUTY-FULL → Full of beauty, pleasing to look at.

meaning: To charm or enchant (someone), sometimes in a deceptive way
exampleSentence: The cunning salesman tried to beguile customers with flattering words.
mnemonic: BE-GUILE → BE full of GUILE (cunning) — to charm deceptively.

meaning: Hostile and aggressive; engaging in war or conflict; warlike
exampleSentence: The belligerent tone of his speech made negotiations even harder.
mnemonic: BELL-I-GERENT → Imagine ringing a BELL to start a war — belligerent means warlike!

meaning: The utterance of a blessing, especially at the end of a religious service
exampleSentence: The priest offered a benediction to the congregation before they departed.
mnemonic: BENE-DICTION → BENE means good (like benefit), DICTION means speech — good speech = blessing.

meaning: Favourable or advantageous; resulting in good
exampleSentence: Regular exercise is highly beneficial to both physical and mental health.
mnemonic: BENE-FICIAL → BENE means good — something beneficial brings good results.

meaning: Well meaning and kindly
exampleSentence: The benevolent businessman donated millions to build schools in rural areas.
mnemonic: BENE-VOLENT → BENE (good) + VOLENT (willing) — willing to do good.

meaning: Cause (someone) to become perplexed and confused
exampleSentence: The complex math problem bewildered the students during the exam.
mnemonic: BE-WILDER → Being in the WILD with no map would leave you confused and bewildered!

meaning: To divide into two branches or parts
exampleSentence: The river bifurcates into two streams just north of the village.
mnemonic: BI-FURCATE → BI means two (like bicycle) — to split into two parts.

meaning: Very strange or unusual
exampleSentence: It was bizarre to see snow falling in the middle of summer.
mnemonic: BIZARRE → Sounds like BAZAAR — imagine a BAZAAR selling only bizarre, weird things!

meaning: Responsibility for a fault or wrong; as a verb, to assign responsibility for a fault or wrong
exampleSentence: The manager tried to blame the intern for the project's failure.
mnemonic: B-LAME → When you BLAME someone, you call them LAME for their mistake.

meaning: Bare and exposed; cold and miserable; not hopeful
exampleSentence: The economic forecast looks bleak with rising unemployment and inflation.
mnemonic: BLEAK rhymes with WEAK — a bleak future looks weak and hopeless.

meaning: Mix together; combine; form a harmonious combination
exampleSentence: The chef learned to blend spices from different cuisines to create unique flavors.
mnemonic: BLEND → Think of a BLENDER mixing fruits together.

meaning: Perfect happiness; great joy
exampleSentence: The couple lived in bliss during the first few months of their marriage.
mnemonic: BLISS → A warm KISS brings BLISS — perfect happiness!

meaning: A large solid piece of hard material; as a verb, to obstruct or impede
exampleSentence: A fallen tree was used to block the road during the protest.
mnemonic: BLOCK → A solid BLOCK of concrete blocks your path.

meaning: (Of a cutting instrument) not having a sharp edge or point; as a verb, to make or become less sharp
exampleSentence: His blunt remarks about her cooking hurt her feelings deeply.
mnemonic: BLUNT → A BLUNT knife can't cut — it's dull, just like blunt words lack finesse.

meaning: Speak with excessive pride; possess (something impressive)
exampleSentence: He would always boast about his high score to anyone who would listen.
mnemonic: BOAST → Like making a TOAST to yourself — excessive self-praise!

meaning: Not genuine or true
exampleSentence: The police discovered the businessman was using bogus documents for his visa.
mnemonic: BO-GUS → Sounds like 'NO GUSTO' — a fake thing has no real gusto or authenticity.

meaning: Noisy, energetic, and cheerful; rowdy
exampleSentence: The boisterous crowd cheered loudly as the team scored the winning goal.
mnemonic: BOI-STEROUS → Think of rowdy BOYS being STEROUS (noisy) at a party!

meaning: Large in quantity; abundant; liberal or generous in bestowing gifts or favors
exampleSentence: The farmer's bountiful harvest was enough to feed the entire village.
mnemonic: BOUNTY-FULL → Full of BOUNTY — generous and abundant!

meaning: Endure or face unpleasant conditions; dare to do something; ready to face danger
exampleSentence: The brave firefighter rushed into the burning building to save the child.
mnemonic: BRAVE → Think of a lion's ROAR — the BRAVE animal of the jungle.

meaning: Bold and without shame; as a verb, to face something boldly or shamelessly
exampleSentence: His brazen disregard for the rules shocked everyone in the office.
mnemonic: BRAZEN → Sounds like BLAZING — someone with BLAZING boldness, no shame!

meaning: Act of breaking or failing to observe a law, agreement, or code of conduct; gap in a wall
exampleSentence: The company was sued for a breach of contract with its suppliers.
mnemonic: BREACH → BREAK + REACH — when someone BREAKs a promise, they've breached it.

meaning: Concise and exact use of words in writing or speech; shortness of time
exampleSentence: The professor was known for the brevity of her lectures — always precise and to the point.
mnemonic: BREV-ITY → BRIEF-ITY — brevity means being BRIEF and concise.

meaning: Short in time, duration, length, or extent; concise
exampleSentence: The manager gave a brief overview of the project before the detailed presentation.
mnemonic: BRIEF → Like a BRIEF case — small and compact, containing only the essentials.

meaning: Active, fast, and energetic
exampleSentence: She took a brisk walk around the park to clear her mind before the exam.
mnemonic: BRISK → Think of a cold BREEZE that makes you walk BRISKLY to stay warm!

meaning: Hard but liable to break easily
exampleSentence: The brittle glass shattered into pieces when it fell from the shelf.
mnemonic: BRITTLE → BRIT + LITTLE — a LITTLE pressure makes BRITTLE things break.

meaning: Savagely violent
exampleSentence: The dictator's brutal regime oppressed the people for decades.
mnemonic: BRUT-AL → BRUTE + ALL — a brute is someone who is violent, all the time.

meaning: Relating to the pleasant aspects of the countryside and country life
exampleSentence: The bucolic scenery of rolling hills and meadows made for a perfect weekend retreat.
mnemonic: BU-COLIC → Imagine a BULL (BU) having a FROLIC in the countryside — that's bucolic!

meaning: Move in an energetic or noisy manner; as a noun, excited activity and movement
exampleSentence: The market was full of bustle as vendors shouted and customers haggled over prices.
mnemonic: BUSTLE → BUS-TLE — imagine the HUSTLE and BUSTLE at a BUS stop!

meaning: Persuade (someone) to do something by sustained coaxing or flattery
exampleSentence: She tried to cajole her brother into lending her his car for the weekend.
mnemonic: CA-JOLE → Imagine a CA (chartered accountant) telling JOLEs (jokes) to flatter clients.

meaning: An event causing great and often sudden damage or distress; a disaster
exampleSentence: The earthquake was a calamity that left thousands of people homeless.
mnemonic: CALAM-ITY → CLAM-ITY — imagine clams screaming in a disaster!

meaning: Showing or having an insensitive and cruel disregard for others
exampleSentence: His callous attitude towards the poor showed a complete lack of empathy.
mnemonic: CALL-OUS → Someone who doesn't CALL to check on others — cold and callous.

meaning: Peaceful and undisturbed; freedom from storm or disturbance; tranquility
exampleSentence: Despite the chaos around her, she remained perfectly calm and composed.
mnemonic: CALM → Think of a CALM lake — still, peaceful, undisturbed.

meaning: Disguise or conceal; protective colouring or disguise in animals
exampleSentence: The soldier's camouflage uniform helped him blend into the forest surroundings.
mnemonic: CAMO-FLAGE → CAMO is short for camouflage — army patterns that help you hide.

meaning: Truthful and straightforward; frank
exampleSentence: The candidate gave a candid interview about the challenges she faced growing up.
mnemonic: CAN-DID → A CANDIDATE who DID tell the truth — candid means honest!

meaning: Bad tempered, argumentative, and uncooperative
exampleSentence: The cantankerous old man yelled at children who stepped on his lawn.
mnemonic: CAN'T-ANKEROUS → Someone who CAN'T agree and is always ANGRY (ANKEROUS).

meaning: Having the ability, fitness, or quality necessary to do or achieve a specified thing
exampleSentence: She is a highly capable leader who has managed teams of over fifty people.
mnemonic: CAP-ABLE → You are ABLE to wear the CAP of leadership — capable!

meaning: Given to sudden and unaccountable changes of mood or behaviour
exampleSentence: The capricious weather changed from sunny to stormy within minutes.
mnemonic: CAPRI-CIOUS → Like a CAPRI goat jumping around unpredictably!

meaning: Attract and hold the interest and attention of; charm
exampleSentence: The magician's performance captivated the entire audience for two hours.
mnemonic: CAPTIV-ATE → To make someone a CAPTIVE of your charm — captivated!

meaning: Relaxed and unconcerned
exampleSentence: He dressed in casual clothes for the informal gathering at his friend's house.
mnemonic: CASUAL → Think of a CASUAL Friday — relaxed, no formality.

meaning: An event causing great and often sudden damage or suffering; a disaster
exampleSentence: The oil spill was an environmental catastrophe that affected marine life for years.
mnemonic: CAT-ASTROPHE → Imagine a CAT causing an ASTROPHE (disaster) by knocking everything off the table!

meaning: Able to burn or corrode organic tissue by chemical action; sarcastically biting
exampleSentence: Her caustic remarks about his presentation embarrassed him in front of the team.
mnemonic: CAUS-TIC → CAUSE + STICK — caustic chemicals CAUSE things to STICK and burn.

meaning: (Of a person) careful to avoid potential problems or dangers
exampleSentence: The cautious driver always checked his mirrors before changing lanes.
mnemonic: CAUT-IOUS → CAUTION-OUS — full of CAUTION, always careful.

meaning: Showing a lack of proper concern; offhand or dismissive; carefree and nonchalant
exampleSentence: His cavalier attitude towards safety regulations eventually led to an accident.
mnemonic: CAVALIER → A knight on a horse (CAVALIER) who rides carelessly — showing no concern!

meaning: To bring or come to an end
exampleSentence: The factory was ordered to cease operations until the safety inspection was complete.
mnemonic: CEASE → CEASE-FIRE means to STOP firing — cease means stop!

meaning: To acknowledge (a significant or happy day or event) with a social gathering or enjoyable activity; honour or praise publicly
exampleSentence: The whole town gathered to celebrate the festival with music and dancing.
mnemonic: CELEB-RATE → Celebrities RATE their parties highly — they love to celebrate!

meaning: Positioned in or relating to the sky or outer space
exampleSentence: The astronomer spent hours observing celestial bodies through his telescope.
mnemonic: CELEST-IAL → Think of CELESTE (sky blue) — celestial means related to the sky or heavens.

meaning: Express severe disapproval of (someone or something), especially in a formal statement
exampleSentence: Parliament voted to censure the minister for his unethical conduct.
mnemonic: CEN-SURE → CENSOR + SURE — when you CENSOR someone, you're SURELY disapproving of them.

meaning: Known for sure; established beyond doubt
exampleSentence: I am certain that the meeting is scheduled for tomorrow morning.
mnemonic: CER-TAIN → CERTAIN sounds like 'SIR-TAIN' — 'Sir, it's certain!' — beyond doubt.

meaning: Distress or embarrassment at having failed or been humiliated
exampleSentence: Much to her chagrin, she realized she had been calling him by the wrong name all evening.
mnemonic: CHA-GRIN → Your GRIN turns into a CHA (sad expression) — embarrassment!

meaning: To make or become different
exampleSentence: The new policy will change the way employees work from home.
mnemonic: CHANGE → Think of CHANGING clothes — making something different.

meaning: Complete disorder and confusion
exampleSentence: The sudden power outage caused chaos in the busy shopping mall.
mnemonic: CHAOS → CHA-OS → Imagine the Operating System (OS) crashing — total CHAOS!

meaning: In a state of complete confusion and disorder
exampleSentence: The chaotic traffic during rush hour makes commuting a nightmare.
mnemonic: CHAOT-IC → Full of CHAOS — completely disordered.

meaning: Not having any sexual nature or intention
exampleSentence: The novel portrays a chaste love story between two childhood friends.
mnemonic: CHASTE → Think of a CHEST that is locked PURE and untouched.

meaning: Rebuke or reprimand severely
exampleSentence: The teacher chastised the student for repeatedly coming late to class.
mnemonic: CHAST-ISE → CHASE + TIES — imagine someone CHASING you to TIE you up as punishment!

meaning: Excessive or prejudiced loyalty or support for one's own cause, group, or gender
exampleSentence: His chauvinism made him believe his country could do no wrong.
mnemonic: CHAUVIN-ISM → Named after Nicolas Chauvin, a blindly loyal French soldier.

meaning: Protect and care for (someone) lovingly
exampleSentence: She will always cherish the memories of her childhood in the countryside.
mnemonic: CHER-ISH → CHERRY-ISH — treat someone like a precious CHERRY — lovingly!

meaning: Scold or rebuke
exampleSentence: The mother gently chided her son for not finishing his homework.
mnemonic: CHIDE → CHILD — you CHIDE (scold) a CHILD who misbehaves.

meaning: (Of a man or his behaviour) courteous and gallant, especially towards women
exampleSentence: The chivalrous knight rescued the princess from the dragon's lair.
mnemonic: CHIVAL-ROUS → Think of medieval CAVALRY knights — gallant and courteous.

meaning: (Of an illness) persisting for a long time or constantly recurring
exampleSentence: She has been suffering from chronic back pain for over five years.
mnemonic: CHRON-IC → CHRONO means time — chronic means lasting a long TIME.

meaning: Rude in a mean-spirited and surly way
exampleSentence: It would be churlish to refuse such a generous invitation.
mnemonic: CHURL-ISH → A CHURL is a rude peasant — churlish means rude and surly.

meaning: (Of a route or journey) longer than the most direct way
exampleSentence: We took a circuitous route through the countryside to avoid the highway traffic.
mnemonic: CIRCUIT-OUS → A CIRCUIT goes around in a loop — circuitous means roundabout!

meaning: Making a loud and confused noise
exampleSentence: The clamorous protest outside the parliament building could be heard blocks away.
mnemonic: CLAMOR-OUS → Full of CLAMOR — loud and noisy!

meaning: A loud appeal or demand
exampleSentence: There was a great clamour among the citizens for better healthcare facilities.
mnemonic: CLAM-OUR → Imagine CLAMS making OUR ears ring with their noise — clamour!

meaning: Kept secret or done secretively, especially because illicit
exampleSentence: The spy conducted clandestine meetings in an abandoned warehouse at midnight.
mnemonic: CLAN-DESTINE → A secret CLAN with a hidden DESTINY — clandestine means secret!

meaning: A violent confrontation; as a verb, to meet and come into violent conflict
exampleSentence: The clash between the two rival groups resulted in several injuries.
mnemonic: CLASH → The CRASH and LASH of swords — a violent clash!

meaning: Awkward in movement or in handling things
exampleSentence: The clumsy waiter spilled soup on the customer's shirt.
mnemonic: CLUM-SY → Imagine someone who CLIMBS messily — they're clumsy!

meaning: Group of similar things or people positioned close together; gather into clusters
exampleSentence: A cluster of stars was visible in the clear night sky.
mnemonic: CLUST-ER → Think of a CLUSTER of grapes — grouped closely together.

meaning: Rough or loose in texture or grain
exampleSentence: The coarse sandpaper was perfect for removing the old paint from the wooden fence.
mnemonic: COARSE → Think of a HORSE with a COARSE mane — rough to the touch.

meaning: Persuade (an unwilling person) to do something by using force or threats
exampleSentence: The bully tried to coerce the younger students into giving up their lunch money.
mnemonic: CO-ERCE → CO + FORCE — to use FORCE together to make someone comply.

meaning: (Of an argument or case) clear, logical, and convincing
exampleSentence: The lawyer presented a cogent argument that convinced the jury of her client's innocence.
mnemonic: CO-GENT → A CO-AGENT (co-gent) who makes a convincing case — clear and logical!

meaning: (Of language) used in ordinary or familiar conversation; not formal or literary
exampleSentence: The essay was rejected because it used too much colloquial language instead of academic writing.
mnemonic: COLLO-QUIAL → COLL (college) + QUIAL (talk) — the casual way students TALK in COLLEGE.

meaning: Extremely large or great
exampleSentence: The company suffered a colossal loss of revenue due to the data breach.
mnemonic: COLOSS-AL → Think of the COLOSSEUM in Rome — a colossal ancient structure!

meaning: Pleasant to look at; attractive
exampleSentence: The comely young woman drew admiring glances as she walked through the garden.
mnemonic: COME-LY → Someone who makes you say 'COME, LY (look) at how pretty!' — comely!

meaning: Causing or meant to cause laughter
exampleSentence: His comic timing was perfect, and the audience burst into laughter.
mnemonic: COMIC → Think of COMIC books — designed to be funny and entertaining.

meaning: Give an authoritative order
exampleSentence: The general issued a command for the troops to advance at dawn.
mnemonic: COM-MAND → A COMMANDER gives a COMMAND — an authoritative order.

meaning: Begin; start
exampleSentence: The ceremony will commence at exactly ten o'clock in the morning.
mnemonic: COM-MENCE → Think of COMMENCEMENT ceremony — the START of a new journey!

meaning: The state or quality of being dedicated to a cause, activity, etc
exampleSentence: Her commitment to environmental conservation inspired many young activists.
mnemonic: COMMIT-MENT → When you COMMIT, you show COMMITMENT — total dedication.

meaning: Feeling dedication and loyalty to a cause, activity, or job; wholeheartedly dedicated
exampleSentence: The committed volunteers worked tirelessly to rebuild homes after the flood.
mnemonic: COMMIT-TED → Someone who has COMMITTED themselves — fully dedicated.

meaning: Occurring, found, or done often; prevalent
exampleSentence: It is common for students to feel nervous before an important examination.
mnemonic: COMMON → COME + ON — 'COME ON, that's COMMON!' — something ordinary and frequent.

meaning: A state of confused and noisy disturbance
exampleSentence: The stray dog running through the classroom caused a huge commotion among the students.
mnemonic: COM-MOTION → COMMON MOTION everywhere — a noisy disturbance with people moving about!

meaning: Closely and neatly packed together; dense; having all the necessary components or features neatly fitted into a small space
exampleSentence: The compact design of the apartment made clever use of every inch of space.
mnemonic: COM-PACT → A PACT to keep things COMpressed — compact means tightly packed.
